speaker: interviewer
text: Survivor's name is George Steinberg. The interviewer is Donna Kenny. Interview is taking place in Long Meadow, Mass. Continue please.

speaker: interviewee
text: [POINTING] This is the picture when I survived. And my sister, what she survived with me, went to Israel. And in Israel was more survivors. And they made there a book, a history. And our pictures is in the book. And this is a picture what's in the book from [NON-ENGLISH]. I will show you the book too, because the same picture is there. This is a girl. I picked her up. Her father was killed. Her mother was killed. And I find out in the time when I was survived-- and there she is a kid. And I promised to God in that time, I will pick up the kid, and I will raise her. And I raised her and adopted her, which I have some papers, adoption papers from Germany. And I adopted her. Then I find out her father's sister was in Israel. And they find out, from connections, I was here. And then they sent somebody and picked her up. I [INAUDIBLE] connections. She was here twice. My son was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name? The girl's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: The name was a Jewish Hebrew name, Aviva. Her name, a maiden name from her-- her maiden name from before when the father's name was Feldman, very famous people in that time in their city.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the woman on the left? [POINTING]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is, on left, my wife, the name. She was survived from here. From my family we survived, three of us. From her family she survived alone. She died two years ago over here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the  boy in the back?

speaker: interviewee
text: The boy, this is my son, what we have over here, his picture's. Gerald, he is over here. And he talked to you also on the same-- in the same area.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is myself. In that time when I was a young human. And now I am old. Still a human.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. When we do this one, we'll just say who's in the back row, who's in the middle row, and who's in the bottom. But we'll try not to touch the picture. They don't want us to touch the pictures.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah I see it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Don't point with your finger.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Don't point. We'll just talk.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is in the back row?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the back row is my brother which is survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the left is your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the left is a lady, which she survived. She's alone. She died this year.

speaker: interviewer
text: The third lady over there?

speaker: interviewee
text: The third lady is my wife, Paula Steinberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's next to her?

speaker: interviewee
text: The next to her is myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who's next to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Next to me is a son from my sister which survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is the woman between?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's the woman next to you? [POINTING] Over here? Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, this is my wife. And this is a survivor, a girl. She's not here anymore. She was living in Baltimore. She survived. And this is [POINTING] my, my sister's husband, which he survived. And they live in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who are the four people, starting on the left? The four people in the middle?

speaker: interviewee
text: The four people-- this is my brother's child, which he survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: And his name was [? Mendel. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: His name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes And his name was [? Ansel ?] the brother's name-- the boy's name. And the next one is my son, which he survived. He's right here. And the third one is the girl, what I took her-- just mentioned in the other name--

speaker: interviewer
text: Aviva?

speaker: interviewee
text: --which I  adopted. Yeah, Aviva. Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is the woman in the middle with the white collar?

speaker: interviewee
text: With the white-- a survivor from our little city. But that time when we were-- and this is from the camps. When we survived, it was in the DP camps. And this is the picture from that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the name of the camp this was taken at?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pocking.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what country?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Germany

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. [POINTS] Can you tell us about this newspaper clipping?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This is a grave from the two little cities where they shoot. From the ghetto, they brought them there, and they shoot them there. And after I survived, I made a monument and a grave with my hand-- our camp. And, and this, I put it in inside. There's a white picture. We don't see it. The amount from the people where they are killed there. Yes. And what-- and the date from the, from the time where they were killed, and the date that we put in the monument. And this is in it-- because now you couldn't read it, because it's very little. And this is the Russian soldiers when they liberated us. And they watch us. And this is survivors and some neighbors. They came to see when we made it for the-- this OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the bottom picture, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: The bottom picture is I made when I came to the United States. And after I got two years or three years, I don't know exactly, I made, in my synagogue where I belong, the names for my brothers and my wife's brothers and sisters and six candles for the six million people from brass. It's still there in the synagogue. And this thing's in the synagogue. And every time when they say, like, you know, the reminder the, the killed, there's the heat from the-- and six candles inside, where we have six candles for the six million people. And this is made from brass. And it's in my synagogue where I belong, which I was there for 30 years. I'm still there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is the man in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: The man? This is myself and my wife on the other side.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Any more?

speaker: interviewer
text: That's it, unless you want to put the book in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you want to shoot the book? Yes? Mister Steinberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes?

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the title page of a book.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me? Read the title please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, this is the-- the name of what we said. This is the name from the, the two little cities. We were together in ghetto and then we gave-- Mlynov, the first name, and Muravica, the second name. And that's what it is, in the beginning. This is mentioned committee-- which not this here-- not necessary-- committee from there, from Israel, where they were in that time when they gave the books. Yeah. OK. So it's a memorial?

speaker: interviewer
text: And this the names from our little [NON-ENGLISH] both cities-- Mlynov and Muravica. Yes. What else? I'll show you some more pictures where they belong over here. Is there a picture of your synagogue?

speaker: interviewer
text: I think it's in the back.

speaker: interviewee
text: [POINTING] No, this is from the street, a street from our little city, from the old city. Somebody had a picture and put it in. Yes. But I want to show you the main pictures, what's-- It's also-- there is not, but I'll-- I'll look for the bigger pictures. These pictures is some I collect in Israel. Some certain survivors had some pictures. These pictures--

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll put it up here on the stand.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, don't point now. Just--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me who's in the top picture, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the top pictures is two, two brothers. They are sons [INAUDIBLE]. Were they killed in the first one? In the ghetto? And this is her daughter, where she was living after the war. She was liberated also, and came to the United States. She died last year. Yeah. And this, the other from the street is a cousin from my wife, from the same city. They find the pictures, and that's why they put them in the book.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know the names of any of these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, his name was Edel. And the second name was Berman.

speaker: interviewer
text: The man at the bottom picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about the people in the top picture, the man on the left with the white hat on?

speaker: interviewee
text: This? [POINTING]

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm hm. Do you know his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name? No. He was a son to the chief rabbi from our city. And this was his brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the rabbi's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the rabbi's name was-- can you believe it? This is in my mind now. Gone.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the woman in the picture, her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. She survived with another Jewish boy in a Czech village, some were Czech [INAUDIBLE]. We survived. And she used to live in the United States when she was married. Matter of fact, she was by my brother, was married to [INAUDIBLE] in the wedding. She died now.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the man--

speaker: interviewee
text: Fine.

speaker: interviewer
text: --at the end of the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: A cousin too.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. All right. Thank you.
